name: Mintlify API
what_it_does: Mintlify provides a REST API and webhook system for programmatically managing API documentation sites, syncing OpenAPI specs, and triggering doc deployments from CI/CD pipelines.
use_cases: Sync an updated OpenAPI spec to a Mintlify docs site on every API release
Trigger documentation deployments from a GitHub Actions workflow after merging to main
Programmatically create or update MDX page content for changelogs or release notes
Receive webhook events when docs are published to invalidate CDN caches downstream
Automate scraping of doc build status to gate release pipelines on successful doc deploys
not_for: Serving dynamic API responses or acting as an application backend
Hosting non-documentation content like marketing pages or e-commerce sites
Real-time collaborative editing of documents like Google Docs or Notion
best_when: Best when you want automated, code-driven documentation updates tied to your API release workflow with minimal manual intervention.
avoid_when: Avoid when your team needs a wiki-style collaborative editor or when docs are entirely static and never need programmatic updates.

auth.notes: API key passed as Bearer token in the Authorization header. Webhook payloads are verified via HMAC-SHA256 signature on the x-mintlify-signature header.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free for open-source projects with public documentation; includes core hosting and deployment features.
pricing.paid_tiers: Startup plan ~$150/month; Growth and Enterprise plans available with custom pricing for private docs, custom domains, and analytics.
pricing.notes: Open-source projects qualify for the free plan; commercial projects require a paid plan for private docs and advanced features.

performance.rate_limits: Not publicly documented; anecdotally permissive for CI/CD use patterns (tens of requests per hour).

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: API errors return standard HTTP status codes with JSON bodies, but error messages can be generic for validation failures.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: OpenAPI spec sync endpoints are idempotent by nature (PUT semantics); page creation endpoints are not.
known_agent_gotchas: Webhook delivery can be delayed by several seconds, making synchronous post-deploy checks unreliable without polling
OpenAPI spec upload has a file size limit that is not clearly documented and may cause silent failures for large specs
API key is tied to an organization and cannot be scoped to a single docs site when managing multiple projects
Deployment status after a spec sync is asynchronous; agents must poll a separate status endpoint rather than relying on the sync response
Rate limit headers are not included in responses, making it difficult for agents to implement adaptive throttling

security_score_components.security_notes: API keys have no expiry or scope restrictions, which increases blast radius if a key is compromised. Webhook signatures use HMAC-SHA256, which is appropriate.
